question_text: Explain why a solid piece of ice floats on liquid water, despite both being the same substance (H₂O).
correct_answer: Ice floats because it is less dense than liquid water. When water freezes, it expands and becomes less dense (approximately 917 kg/m³ compared to 1000 kg/m³ for liquid water). Objects float when their density is less than the density of the fluid they displace.
explanation: When water freezes into ice, the water molecules arrange themselves in a crystalline structure with more space between them than in liquid water. This makes ice less dense than liquid water. Since ice is less dense, it experiences a buoyant force greater than its weight, causing it to float on the surface. This is why density determines whether objects sink or float.
